title: Lohri
intro: Lohri is a popular Hindu and cultural festival celebrated mainly in Punjab, Haryana, and northern parts of India.
description:
Lohri is a popular Hindu and cultural festival celebrated mainly in Punjab, Haryana, and northern parts of India. It marks the end of the winter season and the arrival of longer days after the winter solstice. Lohri is celebrated every year on 13 January, one day before Makar Sankranti, and is closely associated with the harvest of winter crops, especially sugarcane.

The festival is dedicated to gratitude towards nature, the Sun, and the fire element. On Lohri evening, people gather around a bonfire and offer sesame seeds, jaggery, peanuts, rewri, and gajak to the sacred fire. Families and communities celebrate by singing traditional Lohri songs, performing Bhangra and Giddha, and sharing festive foods.

Lohri is also deeply connected with the legendary folk hero Dulla Bhatti, who is remembered in Punjabi folklore as the “Robin Hood of Punjab.” According to popular traditions, Dulla Bhatti lived during the reign of the Mughal emperor Akbar. He is believed to have stood against injustice, protected poor people, and helped girls who were being forced into difficult situations. One of the most famous stories associated with him is that he rescued two girls, Sundri and Mundri, from an unfair situation and arranged their marriages like a guardian. Punjabi Lohri songs often mention “Sundar Mundariye” to honor his bravery, kindness, and protective nature.

Lohri symbolizes gratitude, courage, community bonding, prosperity, and the celebration of new beginnings. It is especially significant for newly married couples and families celebrating the arrival of a newborn child.

Festival Month

Paush (January)

Significance / Importance

Lohri holds great cultural, agricultural, and spiritual importance. Farmers celebrate Lohri as a thanksgiving festival for a good harvest and express gratitude towards nature, the Sun, and the elements that support farming.

The festival also represents the victory of kindness over injustice through the story of Dulla Bhatti. Lohri strengthens family relationships, promotes community harmony, and spreads the message of happiness, generosity, and togetherness.

Puja Vidhi

1. Clean the home and prepare for the evening celebration.
2. Light a sacred bonfire after sunset.
3. Offer sesame seeds, jaggery, peanuts, rewri, and gajak to the fire.
4. Take rounds around the bonfire and pray for prosperity and happiness.
5. Sing traditional Lohri songs and celebrate with family.
6. Share Lohri Prasad with loved ones.

Related Katha / Mantra / Aarti / Chalisa

* Lohri Katha
* Dulla Bhatti Folk Story
* Surya Mantra
* Agni Dev Mantra
* Surya Aarti
* Gayatri Mantra

faq.a1: Lohri is celebrated every year on 13 January.
faq.a2: Lohri is celebrated to welcome the harvest season, honor nature, and express gratitude for prosperity and abundance.
faq.a3: Lohri is mainly celebrated in Punjab, Haryana, and northern India.
faq.a4: Dulla Bhatti was a famous Punjabi folk hero remembered for standing against injustice and helping poor and helpless people during the Mughal era.
faq.q5: Why is Dulla Bhatti associated with Lohri?
faq.a5: Dulla Bhatti is associated with Lohri because Punjabi folk songs remember his bravery and kindness, especially the story of helping Sundri and Mundri.
faq.q6: What is the meaning of “Sundar Mundariye”?
faq.a6: “Sundar Mundariye” is a traditional Lohri folk song that praises Dulla Bhatti and tells the story of his noble deeds.
faq.q7: What offerings are made during Lohri?
faq.a7: Sesame seeds, jaggery, peanuts, rewri, and gajak are offered to the Lohri bonfire.
faq.q8: What is the spiritual message of Lohri?
faq.a8: Lohri teaches gratitude, kindness, unity, generosity, respect for nature, and the celebration of new beginnings.
